title: WebAssembly internals
description: Linear memory, the JS-WASM call boundary, what wasm-bindgen actually does, why string passing isn't free. The substrate the Rust strategy library runs on top of.

options: Each WASM function has its own private heap, allocated by the host.
Linear memory: one big contiguous byte array per module instance, addressable by 32-bit (or 64-bit with memory64) offsets. The host (JS) can read and write the same buffer.
Garbage-collected like the JS heap; same model, same GC.
There is no memory; WASM only has a register file.
questions.explanation: A WASM module owns one Memory object: a byte buffer that grows in 64KB pages, addressable by integer offsets. JS and WASM both read and write the same buffer. The Rust allocator inside WASM (typically the wee_alloc or dlmalloc fork) manages this buffer as a heap; from outside it's just a Uint8Array. This shared-buffer model is what makes serde-wasm-bindgen work: it writes serialized bytes into the linear memory, then JS reads them out.

questions.prompt: What native types can WebAssembly pass directly across the JS-WASM boundary?
options: Every JS type: strings, objects, arrays, functions.
Just i32, i64, f32, f64. Everything else (strings, arrays, structs) is encoded into linear memory and passed by pointer.
Strings and integers; structs are not supported.
Only u8 arrays.
questions.explanation: The WASM ABI is intentionally minimal: four numeric types pass directly. Strings, slices, structs, and JS values cross the boundary by writing bytes into linear memory and passing the pointer + length as i32 arguments. wasm-bindgen generates the glue: on the Rust side, an export that wraps the string with a pointer and length; on the JS side, code that reads the buffer back. The pointer-passing convention is the source of the JS-WASM bridge cost: every string crossing is a malloc + copy + free cycle.

questions.prompt: What does the `#[wasm_bindgen]` macro on a Rust function actually generate?
options: Nothing at compile time; the attribute is just a marker for the wasm-pack runtime.

Two pieces: (a) a Rust trampoline function with a primitive-only signature that wasm-pack exports from the .wasm, and (b) a JavaScript shim that calls the trampoline, marshaling JS values into i32/f64 arguments and back out into JS values.
A C-style FFI declaration with `extern "C"`.
An automatic `serde::Serialize` impl for the function's arguments.
questions.explanation: `#[wasm_bindgen]` is a procedural macro that generates a Rust trampoline (the actual WASM export) plus a JS shim (which lives in the wasm-pack output's .js file). The Rust trampoline takes only WASM-primitive arguments; if the user's function takes a `&str`, the trampoline takes pointer + length and reconstructs the &str from linear memory. The JS shim does the reverse: takes a JS string, allocates linear memory, writes UTF-8 bytes, then calls the trampoline with the pointer + length.

questions.prompt: Why does this site's run_backtest signature use `JsValue` inputs/outputs plus serde-wasm-bindgen rather than typed parameters?
options: Because wasm-bindgen doesn't support struct parameters.

Because shipping a complex input shape (nested objects with optional fields) as individual wasm-bindgen typed parameters becomes unwieldy. JsValue + serde-wasm-bindgen marshals an arbitrary object via the serde Serialize/Deserialize traits, letting us pass `{symbol, bars, strategy}` as a single argument.
Because serde-wasm-bindgen is faster than the typed-parameter path.
Because JsValue is the only legal return type for #[wasm_bindgen].
questions.explanation: wasm-bindgen does support struct parameters (with #[wasm_bindgen] on the struct), but only for `Copy`-able shapes; nested structs with strings get unwieldy. serde-wasm-bindgen is the general-purpose escape hatch: take a single JsValue argument, deserialize it into a Rust struct via serde, do work, serialize the result back to a JsValue. The cost is per-call: walking the object tree and converting on each invocation. For a once-per-request backtest call, this is negligible; for a tight inner loop it would be.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between `wasm-pack build --target nodejs` and `wasm-pack build --target web`?
options: They are identical; the flag is a no-op.

`nodejs` emits CommonJS that uses fs.readFileSync at load time to load the .wasm; `web` emits ESM that fetches the .wasm via the Fetch API. The choice depends on whether your consumer is a Node runtime or a browser.
`web` is the modern target; `nodejs` is deprecated.
`web` emits TypeScript; `nodejs` emits JavaScript.
questions.explanation: wasm-pack outputs different JS glue per target. nodejs uses synchronous fs.readFileSync to load the .wasm bytes at require time. web uses async fetch + WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming. bundler (a third target) outputs an unresolved 'import' that webpack/rollup/Vite resolves at bundle time. This site uses nodejs because the consumer is the Netlify function runtime (Node 22). A browser-side consumer would use web.

questions.prompt: What's the single most-effective Rust-side knob for shrinking the .wasm binary?
options: Adding #[no_mangle] to every function.

Building in release mode with `lto = true` + `codegen-units = 1` + `opt-level = "s"` + `panic = "abort"` + `strip = true` in [profile.release]. Together they eliminate dead code, inline aggressively, optimize for size, drop unwind tables, and strip debug symbols.
Using `#[wasm_bindgen]` on every type.
Compiling with the nightly toolchain.
questions.explanation: WASM binary size is heavily compiler-tuned. lto = true enables link-time optimization across crates (significant deadcode elimination). codegen-units = 1 forces the compiler to consider the entire crate as one unit (better inlining vs. parallel codegen). opt-level = "s" prefers smaller code over faster code. panic = "abort" drops the entire unwind machinery (which is large). strip = true removes debug symbols. This site's Cargo.toml uses all five; the result is ~98 KB for the strategy library.

questions.prompt: Why does this site's Cargo.toml have `wasm-opt = false` in the [package.metadata.wasm-pack.profile.release] section?
options: Because wasm-opt corrupts the output.

Because wasm-pack 0.13 bundles a binaryen old enough that wasm-opt fails on the bulk-memory ops and the i64.trunc_sat_f64_s op that rustc 1.95 emits by default. Disabling wasm-opt skips the optimizer pass entirely; the size cost is small because the [profile.release] settings already do most of the size work.
Because wasm-opt is closed-source.
Because wasm-opt is a Windows-only tool.
questions.explanation: wasm-opt (part of the binaryen suite) is wasm-pack's post-build optimizer. When the bundled binaryen version pre-dates a WASM feature the rustc backend emits, it fails to parse and errors out. Newer Rust toolchains emit bulk-memory ops (memory.copy, memory.fill) and integer saturation ops (i64.trunc_sat_f64_s) by default; older binaryens reject these. Either upgrade the wasm-opt binary or disable the pass. This site disables it because the size win was negligible vs the toolchain-friction cost.

questions.prompt: Netlify's bundler ships JS function code automatically. Why does netlify.toml have `included_files = ["pkg/**"]` for the backtest-background function?
options: Because included_files is the only way to include any files in a Netlify function.

Because the wasm-pack JS glue uses `fs.readFileSync(${__dirname}/backtest_core_bg.wasm)` at module-load time — a runtime path the static-analysis bundler cannot trace. included_files tells the bundler to copy pkg/** into the function bundle regardless of static analysis.
Because the .wasm file is treated as a source-code file rather than a static asset.
Because Netlify caches .wasm files separately from .js files.
questions.explanation: Netlify's function bundler (esbuild-based) traces static imports to figure out what to ship. The wasm-pack output uses `require('fs').readFileSync` with a path built from `__dirname` at runtime, which the bundler can't analyze ahead of time. Without included_files = ["pkg/**"], the bundler would ship the JS glue but not the .wasm sibling, and the readFileSync would 404 at runtime. The included_files entry guarantees the bytes are colocated with the JS at deploy time.
